concern.text: **Item Normalization ignores Modifiers:** "Strip punctuation and sort" sounds fine until you realize DoorDash items are often things like "Build Your Own Burger" where the actual price-driving data is in the sub-bullets (bacon, extra patty, etc.) which your "raw block" parser will likely mangle or ignore. Two "Build Your Own Burgers" can have a $15 price difference. Your matching logic is too shallow to be useful.

reasoning: [Consensus: {'accepted': 2}] Spec only normalizes item strings (lowercase/strip/sort) and the raw format does not include modifier/sub-item lines, so different builds can collapse into the same match. Update parsing to capture modifiers in `items` or force manual/low-confidence inference when modifiers are present.

concern.text: **Date Inference is Statistically Illiterate:** Your algorithm assumes the user provides a perfect reverse-chronological stream. If the user pastes two different blocks of data or misses a month, your "year decrement" logic will hallucinate dates. Worse, defaulting to the "current year" if no explicit year is found is lazy. If I’m parsing my 2024 history in 2026, your script is going to dump everything into 2026 until it hits a lucky "2025" string. It’s a data integrity nightmare.

reasoning: [Consensus: {'accepted': 2}] Date inference assumes reverse-chronological order and defaults to current year when no explicit year is found; there is no validation for pasted blocks or gaps. Add a user-supplied baseline year and ordering validation, and flag ambiguous or out-of-order dates for manual review.

concern.text: **Performance Bottleneck:** You’re using Streamlit, which re-runs the script on every interaction. If a user has three years of data (hundreds or thousands of orders), and you’re re-parsing, re-hashing, and re-running "inference logic" every time they move a price slider, the UI is going to crawl. You haven't mentioned a single word about caching or data persistence beyond the exclusions.

reasoning: [Consensus: {'accepted': 2}] Streamlit reruns on every interaction and the spec only persists exclusions; parsing, hashing, and inference are otherwise recomputed each time. Add caching (e.g., `st.cache_data`) keyed by input file hash so parsing/inference only reruns when data changes.

concern.text: **Brittle Order IDs:** Using `sha256(raw_block)` is amateur hour. You’re relying on the user to copy-paste text from a website perfectly every single time. If DoorDash changes their UI, or the user captures an extra newline, or their browser renders a "•" differently, the hash changes. When the hash changes, all those "manual exclusions" and "price overrides" stored in your JSON file become orphaned garbage. You’re asking the user to do manual labor (price entry) and then giving them a system where that labor can vanish because of a CSS update.

reasoning: [Consensus: {'accepted': 2}] `order_id` is derived from `sha256(raw_block)` and raw blocks can change with minor UI text differences; the spec does not canonicalize the block before hashing. Generate IDs from a canonicalized block (strip review/status lines, normalize whitespace) and/or add a remapping step to preserve prior exclusions.

concern.text: **"Proportional Match" is Nonsense:** Scaling price by item count is a joke. Four $2.00 sodas do not cost the same as two $25.00 steaks. Using a "ratio" to infer prices for $0 orders is basically rolling dice. You’re better off just flagging everything for manual entry than providing "medium confidence" lies that will skew the entire analysis.

reasoning: [Consensus: {'accepted': 2}] Proportional matching uses item count only, which can produce large errors for high-variance menus; the spec does not gate this by price dispersion. Constrain proportional inference to low-variance price bands or replace it with median/restaurant-average, and require manual entry when variance is high.

concern.text: **Outlier Noise:** "2 standard deviations" is a terrible threshold for personal spending. Eating habits are highly irregular. A single fancy group dinner will set a high mean, while a few cheap snacks will create a massive standard deviation. You're going to spam the user with "outlier" flags for perfectly normal Friday night dinners, making the "Outliers Tab" a wall of annoying notifications they'll eventually just ignore.

reasoning: [Consensus: {'accepted': 2}] Outlier logic relies on >2 standard deviations with no minimum sample size or robustness; this can over-flag or fail on irregular data. Add a minimum N, handle zero-variance explicitly, and consider median/MAD with configurable thresholds.

concern.text: **Fragile `order_id` Stability**: The ID is derived from `sha256(raw_block)[:16]`. The Edge Case**: A user reviews an order on DoorDash *after* having already run the visualizer once. DoorDash adds a line like `• Reviewed on 12/14/25` to that order block in the UI. Production Occurrence**: The user copies the updated history into `doordashdata`. Consequence**: The `raw_block` content changes, resulting in a new hash. Any manual exclusions or price overrides previously associated with that order are lost, forcing the user to re-categorize the data.

reasoning: [Consensus: {'accepted': 2}] The spec notes review lines may be added later, but `order_id` hashes the full raw block, so a new review line will change the hash and orphan exclusions. Canonicalize the block to ignore review/status lines or implement a similarity-based remap.

concern.text: **The "Manual Copy-Paste" Foundation:** The entire project is built on the assumption that a user wants to manually scrape a website. This isn't a "Technical Spec"; it's a script that will work for exactly one week until DoorDash updates their frontend, at which point your `parser.py` becomes a collection of broken regexes.

reasoning: [Consensus: {'dismissed': 1, 'acknowledged': 1}] Manual copy/paste is fragile to DoorDash UI changes, which can break parsing; this is a valid risk. The spec explicitly scopes to manual export and a single personal user (Non-Goals: no API sync), so robustness against UI changes is not addressed in this iteration.

concern.text: **Unstable `occurrence_index`**: The ID appends an index to handle duplicate orders (same restaurant/date/items). The Edge Case**: A user has two identical orders for "Sabroso" on the same day. Order A (index 0) and Order B (index 1). The user excludes Order B because it was a duplicate entry error. Later, they realize Order A was also a duplicate and remove its text from the raw file. Production Occurrence**: Manual cleaning of the raw data file. Consequence**: Order B now becomes the first occurrence in the file (index 0). The exclusion record in `state/exclusions.json` is still keyed to `...:1`, meaning Order B is no longer excluded, and a "ghost" exclusion exists for a non-existent index.

reasoning: [Consensus: {'dismissed': 1, 'accepted': 1}] The `occurrence_index` tie-breaker is positional; removing earlier duplicates shifts indices and breaks stored exclusions. Update the ID scheme to use stable tie-breakers or reconcile by similarity so exclusions survive manual edits.

concern.text: **Date Inference - The "Multi-Year Gap" Problem**: The algorithm decrements the year only if the current date is after the previous one. The Edge Case**: A user has a gap in ordering from Dec 2024 to Jan 2026. The file contains "Jan 10" (2026) followed by "Dec 20". Production Occurrence**: Infrequent use of the app over several years. Consequence**: The algorithm assumes "Dec 20" refers to 2025 (the year immediately preceding 2026). It has no mechanism to detect that an entire calendar year (2025) was skipped, leading to a permanent one-year offset for all historical data.

reasoning: [Consensus: {'accepted': 2}] Year inference only decrements when a date would be after the previous one, so multi-year gaps can misassign years; the spec has no gap detection. Add a baseline-year input and flag ambiguous multi-year gaps for manual confirmation.

concern.text: **Date Inference - Leap Year Crash**: The Edge Case**: Parsing a "Feb 29" entry when the inferred baseline year is a non-leap year (e.g., 2025). Production Occurrence**: Processing historical data from 2024 while the script establishes 2025 as the working year. Consequence**: `datetime.date(2025, 2, 29)` will raise a `ValueError`, crashing the entire parsing pipeline and preventing the dashboard from loading.

reasoning: [Consensus: {'accepted': 2}] Parsing Feb 29 against a non-leap year will raise a `ValueError`, and the spec does not mention invalid-date handling. Add a leap-year check and either adjust the inferred year or record a parse error without crashing.

concern.text: **Inference Loop - $0-only Restaurants**: The Edge Case**: A user only ever orders from a specific restaurant using gift cards (e.g., a "work-only" restaurant). Production Occurrence**: All entries for "Sweetgreen" are $0. Consequence**: The "Restaurant average" logic will calculate a mean of $0. The "Proportional match" will find no paid anchors. The system may enter a state where it "successfully" infers a price of $0, providing no value and potentially bypassing the "Manual flag" if the average is technically calculated.

reasoning: [Consensus: {'accepted': 2}] If a restaurant has only $0 orders, restaurant-average and proportional matching can stay at 0 and bypass manual review; the spec does not define a no-paid-anchors fallback. Add a rule: no paid anchors => confidence `manual` and require user input.

concern.text: **Outlier Logic - Small Sample Variance**: The system flags orders > 2 standard deviations from the mean. The Edge Case**: A restaurant has exactly two orders: $20.00 and $20.00. Production Occurrence**: A user frequently orders the exact same meal. Consequence**: The standard deviation is 0. If the user's third order is $20.01 (due to a $0.01 service fee change), the order is technically "infinitely" many standard deviations away. This will either cause a division by zero error in the math or flag every single order as an outlier if there is even a one-cent deviation.

reasoning: [Consensus: {'accepted': 2}] Standard deviation can be zero or undefined for small samples; the spec does not define behavior for N<3 or std dev==0. Add guards to skip the std-dev rule in those cases.

concern.text: **Normalization Collision**: Item normalization involves sorting items alphabetically. The Edge Case**: Order 1: "Large Soda, Small Fries". Order 2: "Small Soda, Large Fries". Production Occurrence**: Different sized items with the same names. Consequence**: If the normalization doesn't preserve the quantity/size association correctly, the "Exact match" price inference might treat these two different orders as identical. If they have different prices, the $0 inference will be inaccurate.

reasoning: [Consensus: {'dismissed': 1, 'accepted': 1}] Normalization steps do not explicitly require preserving size tokens and multiplicity; sorting could be mis-implemented as a set and cause collisions. Update the spec to require multiset comparison of full item strings (including size/quantity text) and no deduplication.

concern.text: **Currency and Locale Hardcoding**: The spec assumes `$` and "Jan 30" (US format). The Edge Case**: A user in a region where DoorDash uses `€` or formats dates as `30 Jan`. Production Occurrence**: International users or travelers. Consequence**: The regex-based parser will fail to find the currency symbol or will misinterpret days as months (or vice versa), leading to "Missing data" errors or corrupted temporal analysis.

reasoning: [Consensus: {'acknowledged': 2}] The parser assumes `$` and US-style dates, which will fail for other locales; this is a valid limitation. The spec targets a single personal user and does not include internationalization, so locale support is out of scope.

concern.text: **Reverse Chronological Assumption**: The date inference relies on the file being in strict reverse order. The Edge Case**: The user's manual copy-paste includes a "Scheduled" or "Active" order that DoorDash places at the top of the UI out of chronological sequence. Production Occurrence**: Copy-pasting while an order is in progress. Consequence**: The "decrement year" trigger (`date > previous_date`) will fire prematurely and incorrectly, shifting the year for every single subsequent order in the file.

reasoning: [Consensus: {'accepted': 2}] Spec's Date Inference Algorithm processes entries in file order (reverse chronological) and decrements year when a missing-year date would be after the previous date. It does not handle out-of-order or active/scheduled entries, so a future/active order at the top would trigger a premature year decrement and cascade. Revise to ignore non-completed orders for the baseline and/or pre-sort by parsed date, or provide a manual year override.

concern.text: **Refund Inflation**: Status lines identify "Refund Issued". The Edge Case**: A $50 order is placed, but later fully refunded due to non-delivery. Production Occurrence**: Common delivery failures. Consequence**: If the status is "Refund Issued", the spec says it is listed separately but "Excluded by default". However, the "Total spending" metrics must explicitly subtract these or ensure they aren't added. If a *partial* refund (credit) is issued, the spec has no schema field to store the *actual* net cost, leading to over-reporting of total expenses.

reasoning: [Consensus: {'accepted': 2}] Spec only stores original `amount_cents` plus `status` and says refunds/credits are listed separately and excluded by default. There is no field for refund/credit amount or net cost, so partial refunds will overstate totals and full refunds rely on exclusion to avoid inflation. Add refund/credit amounts and define totals to use net spending.

concern.text: **Brittle ID Hashing** Quote:** `order_id: str # sha256(raw_block)[:16] + ":" + occurrence_index` ... `Exclusions and price overrides need to survive data file updates.` Simpler Alternative:** A natural key using `date + restaurant + amount_cents + occurrence_index`. Why:** Hashing the "raw block" is a maintenance nightmare. If the user cleans up the source text file (e.g., deletes a "Loved" review line or fixes a typo), the SHA256 hash changes, the `order_id` breaks, and all their manual exclusions and price overrides are lost. Data fields are stable; raw text blocks are not. Requirement that breaks:** The ability to distinguish between two identical orders that have different "review" text in the raw source (which is an edge case that doesn't justify the brittleness).

reasoning: [Consensus: {'accepted': 2}] `order_id` uses `sha256(raw_block)[:16]` to survive data updates, but any non-semantic edit to the raw block (removing a review line, typo fix) changes the hash and breaks persisted exclusions/overrides. That conflicts with the stated persistence goal. Use a canonicalized hash of parsed fields (normalized date/restaurant/items/original_amount/status) or a natural key with duplicate disambiguation, ignoring review-only lines.

concern.text: **Integer Cents in a Data Science App** Quote:** `amount_cents: int # Price in cents (e.g., 5392 for $53.92)` Simpler Alternative:** `float` (Standard Python/Pandas practice). Why:** You are using Pandas and Plotly. These libraries are built to handle floats. By using integer cents, you're forcing every single visualization, filter, and user input to go through a `math.div(100)` or `math.mul(100)` conversion. Floating point errors matter for high-frequency trading, not for seeing how many burritos Jason ate in 2025. Requirement that breaks:** Absolute protection against $0.0000000001 rounding errors in the "Total Spending" summary.

reasoning: [Consensus: {'dismissed': 2}] Simple float storage fails for case Y: range filters, equality/grouping, and totals can misbehave due to binary rounding (e.g., 53.92 stored as 53.919999), which is user-visible in sliders and sums. Patch Z would require quantizing/rounding after every parse and before each comparison/aggregation and display, which spreads across the UI and charts and effectively recreates fixed-point handling. Integer cents are the simpler, centralized fix.

concern.text: **Over-Engineered Price Inference** Quote:** "Price inference logic... 1. Exact match... 2. Proportional match... 3. Restaurant average... 4. Manual entry" Simpler Alternative:** Use a single "Restaurant Average" default for all $0 orders and provide a "Bulk Edit" table in the UI. Why:** "Proportional matching" (scaling by item count) is a guess disguised as math. A "4-item order" could be four $2 tacos or four $25 fajita platters. You are writing complex logic to produce "medium confidence" lies. Just give them the average and let them fix it if they care. Requirement that breaks:** The goal of "estimating costs" automatically for users who have hundreds of gift card orders and refuse to verify them.

reasoning: [Consensus: {'dismissed': 2}] Restaurant-average only fails for case Y: restaurants with wide price variance or repeat identical item sets; averages flatten real patterns and can skew totals when many $0 orders exist. Patch Z would be to segment by item count and/or exact item matches to improve accuracy and assign confidence, which is exactly the staged inference already specified. So the simpler alternative pushes you back into the current logic.

concern.text: **Schema Versioning for a Single User** Quote:** "The schema_version field allows future migrations... run migrations sequentially" Simpler Alternative:** Basic dictionary `.get()` defaults or a simple "reset settings" button. Why:** This is a local script for one person. Sequential migration logic is infrastructure for a SaaS product, not a Streamlit tool. If you change the schema, just update the parser to handle the old key or tell the user to delete their `exclusions.json`. Requirement that breaks:** Seamless, automated data migration if you radically change the JSON structure every week.

reasoning: [Consensus: {'accepted': 1, 'dismissed': 1}] No schema versioning fails for case Y: when the JSON structure changes (renamed fields or new nested overrides), `.get()` defaults cannot recover intent; you need conditional parsing/backfill logic in multiple places or force a delete (data loss). Patch Z is a per-version migration step, which is precisely what a `schema_version` enables and is minimal and centralized.

concern.text: **Sorting as Normalization** Quote:** "Item normalization for matching: ... Sort items alphabetically before comparison" Simpler Alternative:** Direct string comparison or a simple `set()` comparison. Why:** Sorting strings alphabetically to detect matching orders assumes DoorDash randomly shuffles item names in the order history for the same purchase. They don't. You're adding a processing step to solve a problem that doesn't exist in the data source. Requirement that breaks:** Matching "Burger, Fries" with "Fries, Burger" if the source text ever actually flips them.

reasoning: [Consensus: {'dismissed': 2}] Direct string comparison fails if item order changes or whitespace/punctuation differs, and `set()` loses duplicates (two burritos become one). Patch Z requires order-insensitive comparison while preserving multiplicity, which is achieved by normalizing and sorting items (or multiset counts). The spec's sorting step is the minimal patch.

concern.text: **The "Year Inference" Guessing Game** Quote:** "If the resulting date would be AFTER the previously parsed date, decrement the year" Simpler Alternative:** Just ask the user for the "Start Year" of the file in the Sidebar. Why:** Your algorithm is one "Jan 2" order placed out of order (due to a late delivery or status update) away from decrementing the year for the entire rest of the file. Automated date inference is famously fragile. One manual input field in the UI makes this 100% reliable. Requirement that breaks:** The "Success Criteria" that setup takes < 2 minutes without any user input.

reasoning: [Consensus: {'accepted': 2}] Auto year inference alone is brittle when entries are out of order; the spec offers no user override. Add an optional start-year override (or a warning with manual correction) so users can fix mismatches without re-editing the data file.

concern.text: **Existing Data Models (`scripts/models.py`):** The project already utilizes `dataclasses` and a `CostTracker` that handles unit-based calculations (tokens to dollars) similar to the `Order` dataclass's `amount_cents` logic.

reasoning: [Consensus: {'dismissed': 2}] Reviewed `models.py`: `CostTracker` tracks LLM token usage and per-million token rates, not order amounts or per-order analytics. Its fields and math are unrelated to `amount_cents`, so this existing pattern does not apply.

concern.text: **Building a Custom Multi-line Parser** Quote:** "The doordashdata file contains entries like... Multi-line entries may include... Status lines..." Simpler Alternative:** Use a browser snippet to copy the Order History as a clean JSON/CSV from the DoorDash network tab. Why:** You're writing a regex-heavy parser to handle "raw blocks" of text-wrapped copy-pasted data. This is the most fragile part of the system. If DoorDash changes their CSS font size and the text wraps differently, your parser breaks. Requirement that breaks:** The ability to work with a raw copy-paste instead of using "Developer Tools." (But if Jason is using Python 3.9+, he knows how to open the Network tab).

reasoning: [Consensus: {'acknowledged': 1, 'dismissed': 1}] Network-tab JSON export fails for case Y: users who only have copy/paste access from the app/website and want the <2 minute bootstrap; requiring devtools breaks the defined setup flow. Patch Z would be to support both JSON export and raw text copy/paste, which still requires the multi-line parser, so the simpler alternative does not eliminate the parsing logic.

concern.text: **ID Generation Pattern (`scripts/adversaries.py`):** The project has a stable ID generation mechanism (`generate_concern_id`) that uses content hashing to ensure persistence across sessions, which is identical to the proposed `order_id` logic.

reasoning: [Consensus: {'dismissed': 2}] Reviewed `adversaries.py`: `generate_concern_id` hashes immutable concern text for stability. Order data is user-editable; hashing raw text would recreate the brittleness in Concern 3. This pattern does not apply because the underlying content changes.

concern.text: **State Persistence & Versioning (`scripts/gauntlet.py`):** The codebase already implements a robust JSON persistence layer in `.adversarial-spec-gauntlet/` and `~/.adversarial-spec/`, including schema versioning and run indexing.

reasoning: [Consensus: {'dismissed': 2}] Reviewed `gauntlet.py`: persistence targets adversary runs in `~/.adversarial-spec` and `.adversarial-spec-gauntlet` with schemas for concerns, runs, and medals, not per-order exclusions or overrides. Reusing it would couple unrelated domains and does not match the data this app needs.

concern.text: **Redundant Data Modeling & Currency Handling** Existing Pattern:** `CostTracker` in `models.py` already manages the conversion of integer-based units (tokens) into fractional currency (USD) using per-million rates. Relation to Spec:** The spec proposes a new `Order` dataclass and manual price inference logic using `amount_cents`. Alternate Implementation:** Extend the existing `CostTracker` or create a generic `FinancialModel` base class that leverages the existing formatting and accumulation logic. Effort Reduction:** ~15% (Reuse of formatting, accumulation, and summary reporting logic).

reasoning: [Consensus: {'dismissed': 2}] Reviewed `models.py`: `CostTracker` accumulates token counts and costs using model-specific per-million rates and has no reusable formatting/aggregation for fixed-price order data. Extending it into a generic financial model would be a new abstraction, not reuse.

concern.text: **Adversarial Filtering Logic (`scripts/gauntlet.py`):** The "Outlier Management" and "Exclusion" features in the spec (flagging, manual dismissal, persistence) are functional duplicates of the existing `GauntletResult` and `Evaluation` workflow.

reasoning: [Consensus: {'dismissed': 2}] Reviewed `gauntlet.py`: `GauntletResult` and `Evaluation` represent adversary concern adjudication (adversary names, rebuttals, severity). Outlier management is a user-facing data exclusion workflow for orders. The semantics and UI flow are different, so the existing logic is not a fit.

concern.text: **Reinventing State Persistence & Exclusions** Existing Pattern:** `gauntlet.py` manages a `RESOLVED_CONCERNS_FILE` and `runs_index.json`. It handles "Dismissals" (Exclusions) with reasoning, timestamps, and confidence scores. Relation to Spec:** The spec proposes a custom `state/exclusions.json` with `exclusion_reason` and `override_cents`. Alternate Implementation:** Adapt the `GauntletResult` and `Evaluation` schema to handle expense items. The "Outlier Management" UI in the spec is essentially a view of the existing "Evaluation" state. Effort Reduction:** ~30% (Reuse of JSON serialization, schema migration logic, and filesystem management).

reasoning: [Consensus: {'dismissed': 2}] Reviewed `gauntlet.py`: `resolved_concerns.json` stores adversary concern patterns, explanations, timestamps, and confidence. The expense app needs per-order IDs and price overrides. The schemas and lifecycles do not align, so this persistence layer is not reusable.

concern.text: **External API/SDK Integration Patterns** Existing Pattern:** The `integrations/` folder and `litellm` usage provide a clear pattern for wrapping external services (CLI or API) with retry logic and standardized response objects. Relation to Spec:** The spec mentions future DoorDash API sync and price inference. Alternate Implementation:** Structure the `price_inference.py` as an "Integration" similar to `knowledge_service.py`, ensuring it follows existing timeout and error-handling conventions. Effort Reduction:** ~10% (Consistency in architecture and error handling).

reasoning: [Consensus: {'dismissed': 2}] Spec lists DoorDash API sync as a non-goal (manual export only) and places price inference as local logic in `data/price_inference.py`, so no external-service wrapper, retries, or timeouts are needed for this scope. I searched existing integration patterns in `skills/adversarial-spec/scripts/integrations/*.py` and LLM wrappers in `skills/adversarial-spec/execution_planner/llm_extractor.py` using `rg --files -g '*.py'` and `rg -n 'litellm' -S`; these apply to git/process/Context7/LLM calls, not local data inference.

concern.text: **Duplicate Stable ID Generation Logic** Existing Code:** `generate_concern_id` in `adversaries.py` (imported by `gauntlet.py`) already implements `sha256` hashing of raw text blocks to create stable identifiers that survive data updates. Relation to Spec:** The spec proposes `sha256(raw_block)[:16]` for `order_id` to handle persistence for exclusions. Alternate Implementation:** Move `generate_concern_id` to a shared `utils/hashing.py` and use it for both concerns and expenses. Effort Reduction:** ~5% (Standardized hashing and collision handling).

reasoning: [Consensus: {'dismissed': 2}] Spec requires order_id = sha256(raw_block)[:16] + ':' + occurrence_index to keep duplicates distinct; generate_concern_id in `skills/adversarial-spec/scripts/adversaries.py` (found via `rg -n 'generate_concern_id|concern_id|hash' skills/adversarial-spec/scripts`) uses SHA1, adds an adversary prefix, and hashes only concern text, so it cannot represent raw order blocks or occurrence indices without changing semantics. Existing pattern does not apply.

concern.text: **Greenfield Parser Development** Existing Code:** `GauntletConcernParser` (referenced in `debate.py`) and the extraction logic in `models.py` (e.g., `extract_tasks`) already handle block-based text parsing with multi-line support. Relation to Spec:** The spec proposes a new multi-line parser for the `doordashdata` file format. Alternate Implementation:** Implement a `DoorDashParser` as a subclass of an existing (or extracted) `BlockParser` base class to handle the "Date Inference Algorithm" as a specific plugin. Effort Reduction:** ~20% (Reuse of regex patterns for prices/dates and error logging infrastructure).

reasoning: [Consensus: {'dismissed': 2}] I searched for a reusable block parser with `rg -n 'GauntletConcernParser|BlockParser|parser' -S` and inspected `skills/adversarial-spec/execution_planner/gauntlet_concerns.py`; that parser consumes JSON concern arrays and extracts section refs, while extract_tasks in `skills/adversarial-spec/scripts/models.py` parses [TASK]-tagged LLM output. Neither handles the spec multiline `doordashdata` format or date/year inference, so a DoorDash-specific parser is still required.
